name: Microsoft Corporation
shortDescription: The world's largest software company and a leading hyperscale cloud + AI platform: Azure, Microsoft 365, and Copilot, plus OpenAI's largest commercial partner.
businessModel: Three engines. (1) Recurring software subscriptions — Microsoft 365 commercial/consumer, Dynamics, LinkedIn, plus per-seat AI add-ons (M365 Copilot, GitHub Copilot). (2) Consumption cloud — Azure compute, storage, AI inference/training and data services, now the primary growth driver. (3) Transactional/device and advertising — Windows OEM, Surface, search/news ads, Xbox content/services/hardware. AI is sold as premium per-seat SKUs (~$30/user/mo) and as metered Azure AI/OpenAI consumption, with management signaling a shift toward more consumption-based pricing. High-margin software anchors gross margin, partly offset by lower-margin Azure AI infrastructure and rising capex depreciation.
marketContext: Microsoft sits at the center of the generative-AI platform shift. Azure is taking cloud share on AI workloads, reaccelerating to 40% in FQ3 2026 after several decelerating quarters, with an AI revenue run-rate above $37B (+~123% YoY). The defining tension is capital intensity: ~$190B of calendar-2026 capex, well above consensus, driven by capacity and sharp memory/component inflation — raising bear-case concerns about depreciation drag. Strategically, the April 2026 OpenAI restructuring removed Azure exclusivity and IP revenue-share while extending IP licensing to 2032, and the MAI Superintelligence Team (formed Nov 2025 under Mustafa Suleyman) launched seven in-house models at Build 2026 — a push toward AI self-sufficiency.
customers: Global enterprises and governments (Azure + M365 commercial)
OpenAI and other AI labs (Azure compute)

leadership.name: Satya Nadella
leadership.title: Chairman and Chief Executive Officer
leadership.background: Joined Microsoft in 1992; led the Server & Tools / Cloud and Enterprise group before becoming CEO. Architect of the cloud-first, AI-first transformation.

leadership.name: Amy Hood
leadership.title: EVP and Chief Financial Officer
leadership.background: Joined Microsoft in 2002; held finance roles across the Business Division before becoming CFO. Credited with capital discipline through the cloud era.

leadership.name: Mustafa Suleyman
leadership.title: EVP and CEO of Microsoft AI; lead of the MAI Superintelligence Team
leadership.background: Co-founder of DeepMind and Inflection AI; leads consumer AI (Copilot) and the in-house MAI model effort unveiled at Build 2026.

leadership.name: Brad Smith
leadership.title: Vice Chair and President
leadership.background: Chief legal, policy and government-affairs leader; long-tenured executive overseeing regulatory, AI policy, and corporate responsibility.

notes: Trading multiples pulled live via yfinance as of the 2026-06-03 market close (EV/Rev recomputed from close x shares + net debt). All comps share that single close anchor. Qualitative facts sourced to Microsoft IR/SEC, CNBC, GeekWire, Yahoo Finance, and the FQ3 2026 release/transcript.

businessOverview: Microsoft (NASDAQ: MSFT) is the world's largest software company and a top-two hyperscale cloud/AI platform, ~$3.2T market cap (2026-06-03 close).
FQ3 2026 (ended Mar 31, 2026) revenue was $82.9B (+18% YoY); diluted EPS $4.27.
Microsoft Cloud crossed $50B in a quarter for the first time, at $54.5B (+29%).
Azure reaccelerated to +40% YoY (+39% cc), beating the company's own 37-38% guidance.
AI revenue run-rate surpassed $37B annualized, up ~123% YoY; M365 Copilot paid seats topped 20M.
The bear case is capital intensity: ~$190B calendar-2026 capex (~$35B above consensus, ~$25B from memory inflation) sent shares down ~3%.
April 2026 OpenAI restructuring removed Azure exclusivity and IP revenue-share, extended IP licensing to 2032 — framed as margin-accretive.
Seven in-house MAI models launched at Build 2026 signal a push toward long-term AI self-sufficiency.
brief.productModel: Recurring subscriptions (M365 commercial/consumer, Dynamics, LinkedIn, plus M365 Copilot and GitHub Copilot per-seat AI) form the high-margin base. Consumption cloud — Azure compute, storage, and AI inference/training — is the primary growth driver, billed on usage. Transactional/device/advertising covers Windows OEM, Surface, search/news ads, and Xbox. AI monetizes both as premium per-seat SKUs (~$30/user/month) and metered Azure AI/OpenAI consumption, with management moving toward more consumption-based pricing over time.

recentNews.headline: Microsoft FQ3 2026 beats as Azure reaccelerates to 40%; AI run-rate tops $37B
recentNews.whyItMatters: Azure +40% (+39% cc) beat the 37-38% guide and reversed several quarters of deceleration; AI run-rate surpassed $37B (+~123% YoY) and Microsoft Cloud crossed $50B for the first time at $54.5B. The print reframed the AI demand debate in Microsoft's favor.

recentNews.headline: Microsoft guides to ~$190B calendar-2026 capex; stock falls on spending shock
recentNews.whyItMatters: The ~$190B outlook came in ~$35B above the ~$155B consensus, with management attributing ~$25B to memory/component inflation rather than added capacity. Shares fell ~3% after hours on fears depreciation could outrun AI revenue.

recentNews.headline: Microsoft 365 Copilot paid seats surpass 20 million
recentNews.whyItMatters: M365 Copilot paid seats reached 20M+, a ~33% sequential jump from ~15M in January, with seat adds +250% YoY. It confirms accelerating enterprise AI adoption, though ~3.3% penetration shows substantial runway and an unproven full monetization curve.

recentNews.headline: Microsoft and OpenAI restructure their agreement
recentNews.whyItMatters: The restructured deal removes Azure exclusivity and IP revenue-share payments while extending Microsoft's IP licensing through 2032; OpenAI's 20% revenue share to Microsoft continues through 2030 but is now capped. It improves Microsoft's long-term margin profile and frees it to build its own frontier models.

recentNews.headline: Microsoft reorganizes Copilot and consumer-AI org under Mustafa Suleyman
recentNews.whyItMatters: The reorg consolidated Copilot and consumer-AI efforts to accelerate product velocity and prepare for in-house model integration, signaling intent to own more of the AI stack rather than rely solely on OpenAI.

recentNews.headline: At Build 2026, Microsoft unveils seven in-house MAI models, led by MAI-Thinking-1
recentNews.whyItMatters: The MAI Superintelligence Team launched seven models trained from scratch (no distillation), including Microsoft's first reasoning model (MAI-Thinking-1), a 5B MAI-Code-1-Flash for VS Code/GitHub Copilot, and MAI-Image-2.5 — a strategic push toward self-sufficiency and lower long-term inference costs versus OpenAI dependence.

recentNews.headline: Microsoft signals shift toward consumption-based pricing for AI products
recentNews.whyItMatters: Management commentary that consumption-based pricing for Copilot and AI services is coming sooner than expected — a model shift that could expand the monetizable surface beyond fixed per-seat SKUs but adds revenue-timing variability.

recentNews.headline: Investors question whether AI spend will pay off despite the beat
recentNews.whyItMatters: Even after a strong quarter, the debate centers on whether near-doubling capex within two years generates sufficient returns before depreciation pressures margins, keeping MSFT's path tied to sustained Azure/Copilot acceleration.

earningsTakeaways.aiCommentary: AI was the dominant theme. The AI run-rate surpassed $37B (+~123% YoY), and Azure's reacceleration to 40% was driven substantially by AI inference and training demand. M365 Copilot crossed 20M paid seats. Management framed the ~$190B calendar-2026 capex as required to meet AI demand while acknowledging meaningful memory/component inflation. The April 27 OpenAI restructuring (removing Azure exclusivity and IP revenue-share, extending IP licensing to 2032) was presented as margin-accretive over time, and the MAI in-house models underscore a self-sufficiency strategy.
earningsTakeaways.demandCommentary: Demand was broadly positive: Azure consumption growth reaccelerated and was described as constrained more by capacity/supply than by demand, supporting the heavy capex. Microsoft Cloud crossing $50B and P&BP growing 17% pointed to durable enterprise spend, while More Personal Computing was roughly flat (-1%) on a soft PC/legacy backdrop. Management guided next-quarter Azure to 39-40% cc growth, signaling confidence AI-driven demand remains strong into FQ4 2026.

keyRisks: Capital intensity: ~$190B calendar-2026 capex (~$35B above consensus) risks depreciation outrunning AI revenue and compressing operating leverage.
Memory/component inflation: ~$25B of the capex increase is price, not capacity — a sign of cost pressure across the AI supply chain.
Monetization proof: M365 Copilot is only ~3.3% penetrated; the full per-seat-to-consumption monetization curve is unproven.
OpenAI relationship: the restructured deal removes exclusivity and adds OpenAI as a partial competitor as Microsoft builds its own MAI models.
Pricing-model shift: moving Copilot toward consumption pricing adds revenue-timing variability.
Legacy drag: More Personal Computing is flat-to-down on a soft PC backdrop.
Valuation: at 10.1x EV/Rev the recovery path is tied to sustained Azure/Copilot acceleration.

comps.name: Microsoft
comps.note: Azure +40% and a >$37B AI run-rate on premium software margin, offset by the ~$190B capex overhang

comps.name: Alphabet
comps.note: Closest cloud+AI comparable; faster growth at roughly half Microsoft's EV/Rev

comps.name: Amazon
comps.note: AWS is the cloud share leader Azure is closing on; lowest EV/Rev of the group on retail mix

comps.name: Oracle
comps.note: OCI re-rated Oracle to a premium to Microsoft on its AI-cloud backlog

comps.name: Salesforce
comps.note: Apps peer (Agentforce vs Copilot); slowest growth, value multiple

comps.name: Nvidia
comps.note: The AI-infra supplier behind Microsoft's biggest capex line; ~2x the group's EV/Rev

slideBullets: Microsoft FQ3'26: revenue $82.9B (+18%), EPS $4.27, Microsoft Cloud $54.5B (first quarter >$50B), Azure reaccelerating to +40%.
AI is now a >$37B run-rate business (+~123% YoY); M365 Copilot paid seats topped 20M (+250% YoY adds) — but only ~3.3% penetrated.
The market's worry is spend: ~$190B calendar-2026 capex, ~$35B above consensus, ~$25B of it memory/component inflation — shares fell ~3%.
Strategic pivot to self-sufficiency: OpenAI restructuring drops Azure exclusivity/IP share (licensing to 2032) and seven in-house MAI models debut at Build 2026.
Trades at 10.1x EV/Rev (2026-06-03 close) — mid-pack among big-tech peers, below ORCL (12.3x) and NVDA (20.4x) — with a 68.3% software gross margin; the re-rate hinges on Azure/Copilot durability.
diligenceQuestions: How quickly does the ~$190B capex convert to revenue and FCF, and what does the depreciation schedule do to operating margin over FY27-28?
What is the true M365 Copilot monetization curve as pricing shifts toward consumption — net revenue per seat and attach rate trajectory?
Post-restructuring, how does the OpenAI relationship evolve as both partner and competitor, and what does in-house MAI do to inference costs?

rampDemandSignal.headline: Microsoft's GitHub dominates DevOps card spend — 88% adoption, 43% of category spend (8x the #2), and it wins three-quarters of new adopters. A durable demand moat under the developer franchise that Copilot now monetizes.
rampDemandSignal.note: Proxied via GitHub (DevOps), Microsoft's most card-billed product; Azure and M365 are largely committed-contract and under-captured here. Ramp's panel is corporate-card spend across 50k+ businesses and skews SMB / mid-market, so it under-captures committed enterprise contracts.
